key_regimens.name: Dose-Dense AC → Paclitaxel (every 2 weeks)
key_regimens.cycle_days: AC every 14 days × 4 cycles → Paclitaxel every 14 days × 4 cycles (with G-CSF support) [BINV-M 5]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, alopecia, cardiotoxicity (anthracycline), peripheral neuropathy (taxane), fatigue

key_regimens.name: TC (Docetaxel/Cyclophosphamide)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, hypersensitivity, fluid retention, nail changes, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab → AC + Pembrolizumab → Adjuvant Pembrolizumab

drugs.name: Carboplatin
drugs.dose: AUC 5 IV Day 1 or AUC 1.5 IV Days 1,8,15

key_regimens.cycle_days: Cycles 1–4 every 21 days (pembro + carboplatin/paclitaxel), cycles 5–8 (pembro + AC), then adjuvant pembrolizumab every 3 weeks × 9 cycles [BINV-M 5]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immunotherapy-related (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies), neutropenia, alopecia, neuropathy

key_regimens.name: TCHP (Docetaxel/Carboplatin + Trastuzumab + Pertuzumab)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 21 days × 6 cycles, then continue trastuzumab and pertuzumab to complete 1 year [BINV-M 7]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Cardiotoxicity (especially with anthracyclines; TCHP is non-anthracycline), myelosuppression, diarrhea, rash

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant (after preoperative for HER2+ residual disease) or metastatic (HER2+ or HER2-low)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 21 days; adjuvant: 14 cycles; metastatic: until progression [BINV-M 7, BINV-Q 10]
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis (monitor closely, fatal cases reported), nausea, myelosuppression, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic TNBC and HR+ HER2- (after endocrine failure and ≥2 prior chemo lines)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (may require G-CSF), diarrhea, nausea, anemia, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Ribociclib + AI (First-line metastatic HR+ HER2-)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic HR+ HER2- first-line or after progression on prior endocrine

drugs.name: Aromatase inhibitor
drugs.dose: Standard (letrozole 2.5 mg, anastrozole 1 mg, exemestane 25 mg)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, QTc prolongation (monitor), hepatotoxicity, nausea, fatigue

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine/Tucatinib + Trastuzumab
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic HER2+ (second/third line, especially with CNS mets)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Diarrhea, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, hepatotoxicity, nausea, fatigue

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Monitoring
treatment_response_assessment.timing: For preoperative systemic therapy, response should be assessed routinely by clinical exam and imaging (mammogram, ultrasound, MRI) during therapy. MRI is more accurate than mammography for assessing tumor response. Imaging methods should be determined by the multidisciplinary team. Standard regimen should be completed before surgery; if progression, consider alternative systemic therapy or proceed to surgery if resectable [BINV-L 2, BINV-15]. For metastatic disease, monitoring intervals vary by treatment type: chemotherapy every 2–4 cycles, endocrine therapy every 2–6 months. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan every 2–6 cycles or 2–6 months. PET/CT as clinically indicated. Tumor markers (CEA, CA 15-3, CA 27.29) as clinically indicated; rising markers alone rarely used to declare progression [BINV-R 3].
response_logic: For preoperative therapy, pathologic complete response (pCR) defined as ypT0 ypN0 (or ypT0/is ypN0) is associated with favorable prognosis, especially in TNBC and HER2+. Residual cancer burden (RCB) provides prognostic information (category 2B recommendation) [BINV-L 2].

For metastatic disease, use RECIST 1.1 or WHO criteria to define response, stability, or progression. Same imaging modality should be used over time. Functional imaging (bone scan, PET) may show flare in responding disease [BINV-R 2].

Progression is defined by unequivocal evidence: worsening symptoms, declining performance status, new or enlarging lesions on imaging, increasing tumor markers (with caution). Rising tumor markers alone rarely declare progression; clinical correlation needed [BINV-R 1].

For patients on immunotherapy, pseudoprogression may occur; confirmatory imaging before declaring progression may be needed. Manage immune-related adverse events per NCCN guidelines for immune checkpoint inhibitor toxicities [BINV-M 1, BINV-Q 1].
imaging_recommendations: Baseline prior to new therapy: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast; bone scan or sodium fluoride PET/CT; FDG-PET/CT as indicated. Brain MRI with contrast if CNS symptoms [BINV-18, BINV-R 3].

During chemotherapy: symptom assessment, physical exam, PS, weight, LFTs, CBC prior to each cycle. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 2–4 cycles. Bone scan every 4–6 cycles. PET/CT as clinically indicated [BINV-R 3].
During endocrine therapy: same assessment every 1–3 months. CT every 2–6 months; bone scan every 2–6 months. Frequency can be reduced for patients with long-term stable disease [BINV-R 3].

Post-treatment surveillance for early-stage: mammography every 12 months (starting 6–12 months after RT for BCS). No routine imaging for reconstructed breast after mastectomy. No routine laboratory or imaging for metastases in asymptomatic patients [BINV-17].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: For metastatic disease, biopsy of first recurrence is recommended to confirm ER/PR/HER2 status. Re-biopsy at progression should be considered if management may change. Discordance between primary and metastatic sites is well-documented; ER/PgR and HER2 status can change with treatment [BINV-18, BINV-A].

ctDNA (liquid biopsy) for ESR1 mutations at progression after prior endocrine therapy (preferred over tissue). If ESR1 negative and tumor fraction low, consider reflex tissue-based NGS. Tissue biopsy preferred for PTEN homozygous copy loss or TMB-H [BINV-Q 6, footnote w].

For isolated locoregional recurrence, biopsy to confirm and repeat biomarker testing. Surgical resection with negative margins if feasible, followed by RT (if not previously irradiated) or re-irradiation (if feasible). Consider systemic therapy after local treatment (CALOR trial: chemotherapy benefit mainly for ER-negative) [BINV-19, BINV-20, MS-56].

For oligometastatic de novo disease, aggressive multimodality approach (locoregional therapy of primary + ablative treatment of metastases + 'adjuvant-like' systemic therapy) may be considered in favorable cases (HER2+, ER+, limited sites, bone-only, induced NED). Prospective trials have not shown survival improvement but may reduce local progression [BINV-18].

prognosis.overall: Breast cancer is the most common malignancy in females in the US, with an estimated 313,510 new cases and 42,780 deaths in 2024 [1,2]. Prognosis varies widely by stage, subtype, and treatment response. For ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), 15-year breast cancer mortality is approximately 1.7% with breast-conserving therapy (BCT) versus 2.3% with breast-conserving surgery (BCS) alone (HR 0.77; 95% CI 0.67–0.88; P<.001) [29]. For early-stage invasive breast cancer, the EBCTCG meta-analysis demonstrated that adjuvant radiotherapy reduces 10-year recurrence risk from 35% to 19% (RR 0.52; 95% CI 0.48–0.56) and 15-year breast cancer death from 25% to 21% (RR 0.82; 95% CI 0.75–0.90) [117]. Pathologic complete response (pCR) to neoadjuvant therapy is strongly associated with favorable outcomes, particularly for triple-negative (TNBC) and HER2-positive subtypes [256,257]. The 5-year event-free survival (EFS) in the KEYNOTE-522 trial for early TNBC treated with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy was 84.5% versus 76.8% with placebo (HR 0.63; 95% CI 0.48–0.82) [273].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated; the 15-year breast cancer mortality is 1.7% for BCT [29].
by_stage.context: Based on SEER data of 140,366 patients with DCIS [29].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year distant recurrence risk <4% for 21-gene recurrence score 0–10; 9-year DFS with endocrine therapy alone 94% for RS 11–25 in TAILORx (postmenopausal) [353].
10-year DFS 55% with CAF followed by tamoxifen for RS ≥31 vs 43% with tamoxifen alone in SWOG 8814 [350].

prognostic_factors: Tumor size, axillary lymph node status, histologic grade, estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) status, HER2 expression, Ki-67 index, gene expression profiles (e.g., 21-gene recurrence score, 70-gene signature, PAM50, Breast Cancer Index), LVI, age, menopausal status, response to neoadjuvant therapy (pCR, RCB), and germline BRCA1/2 mutation status.
The magnitude of endocrine therapy benefit is dependent on ER expression level and recurrence score [352,353].
For DCIS, risk factors for ipsilateral breast tumor recurrence include larger DCIS size, palpable mass, grade III, margins ≤2 mm, ER-negative, and age <50 [DCIS-1].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognostic information from gene expression assays complements T,N,M and biomarker data. The 21-gene assay (Oncotype Dx) is preferred for both prognosis and prediction of chemotherapy benefit in HR-positive/HER2-negative disease [BINV-N 1]. pCR correlates most strongly with long-term outcomes in TNBC, less so in HER2-positive, and least in HR-positive disease [256,257].

disease_related.management: Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid, pamidronate) or denosumab (category 1) plus calcium/vitamin D; palliative RT; surgery for impending fracture or cord compression [BINV-21].

disease_related.complication: Malignant wounds, skin ulceration, bleeding, fungation
disease_related.management: Palliative surgery, RT, wound care; consider systemic therapy to best response then surgical resection if feasible [BINV-19, BINV-20].

disease_related.complication: Brain metastases (especially in HER2-positive and TNBC)
disease_related.management: See NCCN Guidelines for CNS Cancers; consider SRS, WBRT, surgery. Tucatinib + capecitabine + trastuzumab preferred for CNS progression in HER2+ disease [BINV-Q 4].
Educate, monitor, refer for lymphedema management (see NCCN Survivorship: Lymphedema). Baseline arm measurements recommended [BINV-E, BINV-17].
Urgent MRI, spine MRI with contrast if back pain/symptoms; RT, surgery, corticosteroids [BINV-18].

chemotherapy: Taxane-induced peripheral neuropathy (TIPN): Higher risk in African descent and diabetes; consider docetaxel as preferred taxane based on lower TIPN [BINV-M 1]. Cryotherapy of hands/feet and glove constriction may reduce risk [1,2].
Anthracycline cardiotoxicity: Regular cardiac monitoring of LVEF (see NCCN Survivorship: Cardiovascular Disease Risk Assessment) [BINV-M 1].
Febrile neutropenia: G-CSF support (see NCCN Hematopoietic Growth Factors) for dose-dense regimens [BINV-M 5, BINV-M 6].
Nausea/vomiting: Antiemetic protocols per NCCN Antiemesis guidelines [BINV-28].
immunotherapy: Immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) toxicities: Screen for and manage ICI-related toxicities including hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, pneumonitis, colitis (see NCCN Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities) [BINV-M 1, BINV-Q 1].
Pembrolizumab when used in KEYNOTE-522 regimen: Common toxicities include rash, diarrhea, colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis [BINV-M 3].
targeted_therapy: HER2-targeted therapy (trastuzumab ± pertuzumab): LVEF monitoring every 3 months during therapy. Increased risk of CHF, especially with anthracycline co-administration [BINV-M 4, BINV-Q 1].
CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib): Neutropenia, leukopenia, diarrhea (abemaciclib), QTc prolongation (ribociclib). Monitor CBCs, LFTs, ECG [BINV-P, BINV-Q].
PARP inhibitors (olaparib, talazoparib): Fatigue, nausea, anemia; monitor renal function [BINV-Q 14].
Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (T-DXd): Interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis; regular monitoring required. Incidence 12% in DESTINY-Breast04 [DB-04] and 11% in DB-06 [560,561].
PI3K pathway inhibitors (alpelisib, inavolisib, capivasertib): Hyperglycemia, rash, diarrhea, stomatitis; monitor glucose [519,510,520].
surgery: Lymphedema after ALND or axillary RT: Incidence 23% with ALND vs 11% with axillary RT in AMAROS trial at 5 years [154].
Mastectomy flap necrosis, wound healing issues: Smoking and obesity increase risk [BINV-H 6].
Breast implant-associated ALCL (BIA-ALCL) and BIA-SCC: Rare, associated with textured implants (ALCL) and both smooth and textured (SCC) [BINV-H 6].
radiation: Bolus should be used for skin involvement, positive superficial margins, dermal lymphatic invasion/extensive LVI [BINV-I 2].
Cardiac toxicity: left-sided RT, anthracycline use increase risk; DIBH technique can reduce cardiac dose [BINV-I 1].
Radiation dermatitis, fatigue, pneumonitis, lymphedema (if nodal RT), brachial plexopathy (rare) [BINV-I].
Breast fibrosis, telangiectasia, shrinkage—more common with conventional fractionation vs hypofractionation [173].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care should be integrated throughout the continuum of breast cancer care. This includes management of symptoms from the disease (e.g., pain, dyspnea, malignant wounds) and from treatment (e.g., neutropenia, nausea, peripheral neuropathy, cardiotoxicity, fatigue, depression, and lymphedema). Early involvement of palliative care is recommended for advanced disease. Regular screening for distress, anxiety, depression, and fear of recurrence is essential. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management, Survivorship, Palliative Care, and Supportive Care [BINV-28].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Encourage healthy diet, active lifestyle, limited alcohol intake, and maintaining ideal body weight (BMI 20–25) to optimize outcomes [BINV-17]. For patients with metastatic disease, address anorexia, cachexia, and malnutrition (see NCCN Palliative Care). Supplemental calcium and vitamin D needed with bisphosphonate/denosumab therapy [BINV-21].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis. For chemotherapy, use appropriate antiemetic regimens based on emetogenicity (e.g., NK1 inhibitors, 5-HT3 antagonists, steroids). Avoid NK1 inhibitors during pregnancy [PREG-2]. Ondansetron and metoclopramide are considered safe during pregnancy [PREG-2].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Primary prophylaxis with G-CSF is recommended for dose-dense chemotherapy regimens (e.g., dose-dense AC → paclitaxel) and for regimens with high febrile neutropenia risk. See NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors [BINV-M 5, BINV-M 6].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Consider VTE risk, especially when combining abemaciclib with tamoxifen [BINV-K 3]. For hospitalized patients with cancer, use prophylactic anticoagulation per guidelines. There is no specific VTE prophylaxis recommendation for ambulatory breast cancer patients in the source.
supportive_care.pain_management: Follow NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. For bone metastases, use bisphosphonates/denosumab, palliative RT, and analgesics. For neuropathic pain from TIPN, consider dose reduction or alternative agents (e.g., docetaxel instead of paclitaxel for high-risk patients) [BINV-M 1].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Screen for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer at initial diagnosis and periodically. Assess for anxiety, depression, fear of progression, PTSD, and social determinants of health. Provide resources through financial navigators, social workers, and support groups. For young patients, address fertility and sexual health concerns [BINV-1, BINV-17, BINV-28].
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental examination with preventive dentistry is required before initiating bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy to reduce risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ). Avoid invasive dental procedures during therapy if possible [BINV-17, BINV-21].

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For patients treated with curative intent (DCIS or early-stage invasive), follow-up includes regular history and physical examination, mammography, and management of treatment-related side effects. For DCIS: history and physical exam every 6–12 months for 5 years, then annually; first mammogram 6–12 months after RT (or after BCS if no RT) then annually [DCIS-2]. For invasive: history and physical exam 1–4 times per year for 5 years, then annually; mammogram every 12 months starting 6–12 months after RT [BINV-17]. No routine lab or imaging for metastases in asymptomatic patients [BINV-17]. Educate patients on lymphedema monitoring, healthy lifestyle, and endocrine therapy adherence [BINV-17].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The primary goals of surveillance are early detection of in-breast recurrences (either DCIS or invasive) and second primary breast cancers, as well as management of long-term treatment effects. Approximately half of DCIS recurrences are invasive. Annual mammography is the standard; no clear benefit to shorter intervals [BINV-17]. Metastatic surveillance with routine labs or imaging has no survival benefit and is not recommended [110,429-432].
late_effects_screening: Bone health: Baseline and periodic bone mineral density (BMD) for patients on aromatase inhibitors or ovarian failure secondary to treatment [BINV-17].
Cardiotoxicity: For patients who received left-sided RT, anthracyclines, or HER2-targeted therapy, monitor LVEF and cardiac risk (see NCCN Survivorship) [BINV-17].
Lymphedema: Pretreatment arm measurements and patient education; refer for lymphedema management as needed [BINV-E, BINV-17].
Endometrial cancer: For patients on tamoxifen, yearly gynecologic assessment and rapid evaluation of vaginal spotting; routine endometrial biopsy/ultrasound not recommended [BINV-17].
Second primary breast cancer: Consider annual MRI for those with high lifetime risk (>20% based on family history) or diagnosed at age ≤50 or dense breasts per ACR guidelines [BINV-B].
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most DCIS recurrences occur in the ipsilateral breast near the original site; approximately half are invasive [DCIS-1]. For invasive breast cancer, recurrences can be locoregional (in-breast, chest wall, axillary, supraclavicular) or distant. Distant metastases are most common in bone, liver, lung, and brain. Inflammatory breast cancer has high rates of locoregional and distant recurrence. For isolated locoregional recurrence, complete surgical resection with clear margins ± RT is recommended; adjuvant chemotherapy improves OS in ER-negative disease per CALOR trial [467].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.frequency: History and physical exam every 6–12 months (DCIS: 6–12 months; invasive: 1–4 times per year as clinically appropriate), then annually after 5 years.
Every 12 months, starting 6–12 months after completion of RT (for BCS) or after BCS if no RT.
Periodic screening for changes in family history and genetic testing indications.
Baseline and periodically (e.g., every 1–2 years depending on risk) for patients on AI or with ovarian failure.

imaging_strategy.schedule: Annual mammogram; first post-treatment mammogram 6–12 months after RT, then annually. Suspicious findings warrant immediate workup. Routine imaging of reconstructed breast after mastectomy not indicated [BINV-17].

Not routinely indicated for follow-up; consider for those with high lifetime risk (>20% based on family history) or personal history of breast cancer diagnosed at ≤50 years or dense breasts per ACR recommendations [BINV-B].

imaging_strategy.modality: Staging imaging (CT, bone scan, PET/CT) for metastases
imaging_strategy.schedule: Not recommended for asymptomatic patients. Only if signs/symptoms of recurrence [BINV-17].

laboratory_monitoring.schedule: Not routinely indicated for metastatic surveillance in asymptomatic patients. If patient on tamoxifen, no routine labs; on AI, consider periodic LFTs and renal function as indicated.
Not recommended for routine surveillance. An isolated increase should rarely be used to declare progression [BINV-R].

supportive_follow_up: Assess and encourage adherence to adjuvant endocrine therapy [BINV-17].
Educate about active lifestyle, healthy diet, limited alcohol, ideal body weight (BMI 20–25) [BINV-17].
Monitor for hormonal therapy side effects: hot flashes, mood changes, arthralgias; consider venlafaxine for hot flashes [332-335].
Provide psychosocial support: screen for distress, fear of recurrence, depression; refer to mental health professionals as needed [BINV-17].
Coordinate care between PCP and specialists; provide survivorship treatment plan including summary of possible long-term toxicities [BINV-17].
For premenopausal patients: fertility counseling, non-hormonal birth control, and sexual health considerations [BINV-C].

special_situations.situation: Breast Cancer in Males (Sex Assigned at Birth)
special_situations.key_message: Male breast cancer is managed similarly to female breast cancer, with special considerations for genetics, endocrine therapy, and use of assays. All males should be offered genetic testing.
special_situations.management: Tamoxifen for 5–10 years is preferred; single-agent AI is not recommended (inferior outcomes). If AI indicated, give with GnRH analog. SLNB is standard for node-negative axilla. BCS outcomes equivalent to mastectomy. Indications for RT and systemic therapy same as for females [BINV-J].
special_situations.evidence: Extrapolated from female trials; limited data for CDK4/6 inhibitors, mTOR inhibitors, and PIK3CA inhibitors in males, but real-world data suggest comparable safety/efficacy. 21-gene assay provides prognostic info [BINV-J].

special_situations.key_message: Chemotherapy should not be administered in the first trimester. RT is contraindicated during any trimester. Anti-HER2 therapy, CDK4/6 inhibitors, ADCs, endocrine therapy, PARP inhibitors, and ICIs are contraindicated.
special_situations.management: Doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide every 3 weeks is preferred; weekly paclitaxel may be used. Avoid chemotherapy after 35 weeks. BCS can be considered if RT can be delayed postpartum. Mastectomy preferred for first-trimester diagnosis to avoid delay in RT. SLNB can be considered with radiolabeled tracer; blue dye is contraindicated [PREG-1, PREG-2].
special_situations.evidence: Based on case series, retrospective studies, and expert opinion. Summary of safety data from Gwyn and Hahn [698,701].

special_situations.key_message: Phyllodes tumors are rare fibroepithelial neoplasms treated by wide local excision with ≥1 cm margins. Axillary staging is not needed unless clinically positive nodes.
special_situations.management: Core biopsy often inadequate; excisional biopsy recommended for diagnosis. Lumpectomy/partial mastectomy with clear margins (≥1 cm). Mastectomy if margins cannot be cleared. No role for RT for positive margins. Systemic therapy for recurrence follows NCCN Soft Tissue Sarcoma guidelines. ER/PR may be expressed but endocrine therapy has no proven role [PHYLL-1].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective studies and expert consensus; no randomized trials.

special_situations.key_message: Paget disease presents as eczematous changes of the nipple/areola; associated with underlying breast cancer (DCIS or invasive) in 80–90% of cases.
special_situations.management: Full-thickness skin biopsy of involved NAC. Breast MRI recommended to define extent. Central lumpectomy including NAC ± SLNB with whole breast RT is acceptable; total mastectomy also an option. Systemic therapy based on stage and biomarker status of underlying cancer [PAGET-1, PAGET-2].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective series; no randomized trials. Outcomes with breast conservation and RT similar to standard BCT [670-674].

special_situations.key_message: IBC is a clinical diagnosis (erythema, edema/peau d'orange ≥1/3 of breast, rapid onset ≤6 months). Requires multimodality treatment: neoadjuvant systemic therapy → mastectomy → chest wall and comprehensive RNI.
special_situations.management: Preoperative anthracycline-based ± taxane chemotherapy ± trastuzumab/pertuzumab for HER2+. Mastectomy with level I/II ALND (skin-sparing and nipple-sparing contraindicated). Delayed reconstruction only. RT to chest wall and comprehensive RNI. Endocrine therapy if HR-positive. No BCS except in highly selected cases with complete response [IBC-1, IBC-2].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective data, small prospective studies. Neoadjuvant trastuzumab improves outcomes [414]. TRYPHAENA and NeoSphere trials included IBC patients [270,271].

special_situations.situation: Occult Primary Breast Cancer Presenting as Axillary Metastases
special_situations.key_message: Workup includes mammogram, ultrasound, breast MRI. If MRI positive, treat as per clinical stage. If MRI negative, options: mastectomy + ALND or ALND + whole breast RT + nodal irradiation.
special_situations.management: Multidisciplinary approach. Systemic therapy per stage II/III disease. Neoadjuvant therapy for cN2–3. ER/PR and HER2 testing on biopsy is critical [BINV-1, BINV-18].

special_situations.key_message: Optimal management remains uncertain. Locoregional treatment of primary tumor and ablative therapy for metastases have not demonstrated improved survival in prospective trials but may reduce local progression and palliate symptoms.
special_situations.management: Consider aggressive multimodality approach for select patients with favorable features (HER2+ or ER+, induced NED after optimal systemic therapy, limited sites, bone-only, young age). Individualize decisions with multidisciplinary team [BINV-18].
special_situations.evidence: Case series; RCTs (eg, Badwe et al. [475], Turkish MF07-01 [478]) show mixed results; survival benefit seen in some subgroups.

clinical_pearls: For patients with HR-positive/HER2-negative early breast cancer, the 21-gene recurrence score (Oncotype Dx) is the preferred assay to determine prognosis and predict chemotherapy benefit. For node-negative patients with RS ≤25, chemotherapy can be omitted in postmenopausal women; in premenopausal women with RS 16–25, a small benefit may exist and ovarian suppression effects should be considered [353,358].

For patients with node-positive (1–3 nodes) HR-positive/HER2-negative disease and RS ≤25, the RxPONDER trial showed chemotherapy benefit only in premenopausal women; postmenopausal women derived no benefit [358].

In HER2-positive early breast cancer, the addition of pertuzumab to trastuzumab plus chemotherapy significantly improves invasive DFS in node-positive patients (APHINITY, 11.3-year follow-up: HR 0.72; 95% CI 0.60–0.87) [MM].

For HER2-positive residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy, adjuvant T-DM1 (KATHERINE) significantly reduces recurrence risk (3-year iDFS 88.3% vs 77%; HR 0.50; 95% CI 0.39–0.64) [410]. Alternatively, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is now a category 1 option for high-risk patients (DESTINY-Breast05 criteria) [BINV-16].

For early triple-negative breast cancer (stage II–III), the KEYNOTE-522 regimen (carboplatin + paclitaxel → doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide + pembrolizumab preoperatively, then adjuvant pembrolizumab) is preferred. At 5 years, EFS rate 81.3% vs 72.3% (HR 0.63; 95% CI 0.49–0.81) [274].

Treatment de-escalation in older adults: Patients ≥70 years with HR-positive, HER2-negative, cN0, pT1 tumors can have RT omitted after BCS if endocrine therapy is planned (category 1) [BINV-2]. Patients >50 years, postmenopausal, with cT1N0, HR+/HER2-negative, grade 1–2 tumors (node-negative by axillary ultrasound) may be considered for omission of SLNB [BINV-1].

Axillary management: For patients with cT1–2, cN0, 1–2 positive SLNs undergoing BCS with WBRT, ALND can be omitted (ACOSOG Z0011). For mastectomy patients with 1–2 positive SLNs, axillary RT may replace ALND (AMAROS) [BINV-D].

For metastatic HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer, first-line endocrine therapy plus CDK4/6 inhibitor is preferred over chemotherapy regardless of visceral involvement, unless visceral crisis. Ribociclib has demonstrated OS benefit in MONALEESA-2 and -7 [502,503]. Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan is now an option for HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative) patients after endocrine therapy (DESTINY-Breast06) [560].

For metastatic triple-negative breast cancer with PD-L1 CPS ≥10, first-line therapy with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-355) or sacituzumab govitecan + pembrolizumab (ASCENT-04) is category 1 preferred [597,599]. For PD-L1 CPS <10, sacituzumab govitecan (ASCENT-03) is preferred [600].

For patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations and metastatic HER2-negative breast cancer, PARP inhibitors (olaparib or talazoparib) are category 1 preferred options, especially in earlier lines of therapy [643,645].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (NCCN Guidelines®) Breast Cancer, Version 5.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive guideline covering DCIS, invasive breast cancer, inflammatory breast cancer, Paget disease, phyllodes tumors, male breast cancer, and breast cancer during pregnancy. Provides recommendations for screening, diagnosis, staging, treatment (surgery, radiation, systemic therapy), surveillance, and survivorship. Includes panel consensus and evidence categories.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition – Breast Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines T, N, M categories; anatomic and prognostic stage groups (clinical and pathological). Includes genomic profile (Oncotype Dx <11) in pathological prognostic staging.

guidelines_resources.organization: American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) / College of American Pathologists (CAP)
guidelines_resources.title: ASCO/CAP HER2 Testing Guideline (2023) and ER/PR Testing Guideline (2020)
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides methodology for HER2, ER, and PR testing by IHC and ISH; defines positive, equivocal, and negative categories; recommends testing on all new primary and metastatic breast cancers.

guidelines_resources.organization: Society of Surgical Oncology (SSO) / American Society for Radiation Oncology (ASTRO) / ASCO
guidelines_resources.title: SSO/ASTRO/ASCO Consensus Guideline on Margins for Breast-Conserving Surgery (2014 for invasive, 2016 for DCIS)
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines 'no ink on tumor' as adequate margin for invasive cancer; ≥2 mm margin for DCIS when whole breast RT is planned.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Breast Cancer Risk Reduction
guidelines_resources.notes: Recommendations for risk-reducing interventions including endocrine therapy, lifestyle modifications, and risk-reducing mastectomy.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Criteria for genetic testing (including all male breast cancer), management of BRCA1/2 and other hereditary syndromes.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
guidelines_resources.notes: Long-term follow-up, late effects screening, lymphedema management, cardiotoxicity monitoring, psychosocial support, and fertility preservation.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management, Antiemesis, Hematopoietic Growth Factors, Palliative Care, Adult Cancer Pain, and Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced throughout the breast cancer guideline for symptom management and supportive care.

key_trials.full_name: Trial Assigning IndividuaLized Options for Treatment (Rx)
key_trials.population: HR-positive, HER2-negative, node-negative breast cancer with 21-gene recurrence score 11–25
key_trials.intervention: Chemoendocrine therapy (chemotherapy followed by endocrine therapy)
key_trials.key_result: Among postmenopausal patients with RS 11–25, 9-year DFS was 94% in both arms (HR 1.06; 95% CI 0.93–1.20). In premenopausal patients with RS 16–25, chemotherapy benefit was seen (HR for distant recurrence 0.44; 95% CI 0.20–0.96) [353].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS similar; benefit in premenopausal patients with RS 16–25 for distant recurrence.
key_trials.practice_change: Established that adjuvant chemotherapy can be omitted in postmenopausal node-negative patients with RS 11–25; in premenopausal patients with RS 16–25, chemotherapy (with associated ovarian suppression) may provide benefit.

key_trials.full_name: Rx for Positive Node, Endocrine Responsive Breast Cancer (SWOG S1007)
key_trials.key_result: In premenopausal patients, iDFS improved with chemotherapy (HR 0.6; 95% CI 0.43–0.83). In postmenopausal patients, no benefit (HR 1.02; 95% CI 0.82–1.26) [358].
key_trials.practice_change: Chemotherapy can be omitted in postmenopausal women with 1–3 positive nodes and RS ≤25; premenopausal women still benefit and should receive chemotherapy.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab in Early Triple-Negative Breast Cancer
key_trials.journal: New England Journal of Medicine (2020), Annals of Oncology (2023), JAMA (2025?)
key_trials.population: Previously untreated stage II–III triple-negative breast cancer
key_trials.intervention: Preoperative carboplatin + paclitaxel → cyclophosphamide/doxorubicin (epirubicin) + pembrolizumab, then adjuvant pembrolizumab
key_trials.comparator: Preoperative chemotherapy + placebo, then adjuvant placebo
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy as standard neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy for high-risk TNBC.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Olaparib in Germline BRCA–Mutated Early High-Risk Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: gBRCA1/2 mutation, high-risk HER2-negative early breast cancer (≥pT2 or ≥pN1, residual disease after neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy)
key_trials.practice_change: Adjuvant olaparib is standard for high-risk gBRCA-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer after completion of (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy.

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan vs Treatment of Physician's Choice in HER2-Low Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/ISH negative) metastatic breast cancer, previously treated with 1–2 chemotherapy lines (HR+ patients also received endocrine therapy)
key_trials.comparator: Physician's choice chemotherapy (capecitabine, eribulin, gemcitabine, paclitaxel, nab-paclitaxel)
key_trials.primary_endpoint: PFS (in HR-positive cohort) and overall PFS
key_trials.key_result: In HR-positive: median PFS 10.1 vs 5.4 months (HR 0.51; 95% CI 0.40–0.64); OS 23.9 vs 17.6 months (HR 0.69; 95% CI 0.55–0.87). In all patients: PFS 9.9 vs 5.1 months (HR 0.50). ILD 12% (0.8% fatal) [560].
key_trials.practice_change: Established T-DXd as a new standard for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer, including HR-positive disease after endocrine therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Pertuzumab, Trastuzumab, and Docetaxel in HER2-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 18.5 vs 12.4 months (HR 0.62; 95% CI 0.51–0.75). OS benefit: HR 0.66; 95% CI 0.52–0.84 at 30-month follow-up [564,565].
key_trials.practice_change: Established THP as standard first-line therapy for HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Pertuzumab and Trastuzumab in Early HER2-Positive Breast Cancer
key_trials.journal: New England Journal of Medicine, Annals of Oncology
key_trials.population: Node-positive or high-risk node-negative HER2-positive early breast cancer
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant chemotherapy + trastuzumab + pertuzumab for 1 year
key_trials.comparator: Adjuvant chemotherapy + trastuzumab + placebo for 1 year
key_trials.key_result: At 8.4 years: iDFS 86% vs 81% in node-positive (HR 0.72; 95% CI 0.60–0.87); no benefit in node-negative [408,409].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS not significant; cardiac safety acceptable.
key_trials.practice_change: Pertuzumab added to chemotherapy + trastuzumab is standard for node-positive HER2-positive early breast cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Emtansine for Residual Invasive HER2-Positive Early Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-positive early breast cancer with residual invasive disease after neoadjuvant chemotherapy + HER2-targeted therapy
key_trials.practice_change: Standard of care for HER2-positive residual disease after neoadjuvant therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Ribociclib plus Letrozole in First-Line for HR+/HER2- Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: Postmenopausal, HR-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer, no prior therapy for advanced disease
key_trials.practice_change: Established ribociclib + letrozole as standard first-line therapy; ribociclib is the only CDK4/6 inhibitor with OS benefit in first line.

key_trials.full_name: Sacituzumab Govitecan vs Chemotherapy in Untreated Advanced TNBC
key_trials.population: Previously untreated locally advanced unresectable or metastatic TNBC (PD-L1 CPS <10 or not candidate for PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor)
key_trials.comparator: Physician's choice chemotherapy (paclitaxel, nab-paclitaxel, carboplatin/gemcitabine, eribulin, capecitabine)
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 9.7 vs 6.9 months (HR 0.62; 95% CI 0.51–0.75). OS data immature; PFS2 benefit (HR 0.70; 95% CI 0.55–0.90) [600].
key_trials.practice_change: Sacituzumab govitecan is a category 1 preferred option for first-line TNBC without PD-L1 CPS ≥10.

key_trials.full_name: Imlunestrant ± Abemaciclib in HR+/HER2- Advanced Breast Cancer
key_trials.population: ER-positive/HER2-negative advanced breast cancer after progression on AI ± CDK4/6i
key_trials.intervention: Imlunestrant (oral SERD) ± abemaciclib, or imlunestrant alone
key_trials.key_result: Imlunestrant alone improved PFS vs standard therapy only in ESR1-mutant tumors (5.5 vs 3.8 months; HR 0.62; 95% CI 0.44–0.82). Imlunestrant + abemaciclib improved PFS vs imlunestrant alone (9.4 vs 5.5 months; HR 0.57; 95% CI 0.44–0.73) [530].
key_trials.practice_change: Imlunestrant is an option for ESR1-mutant advanced breast cancer after prior CDK4/6i; combination with abemaciclib may be most effective in ESR1-mutated, CDK4/6i-naïve patients.
